schools.summary: Ellicott, Clarke and Gill fix the season and setting: 'at the return of the year,' the spring, when the army returned to besiege Rabbah and waste Ammon.
Henry reads the fatal clause — David 'tarried still at Jerusalem': out of the way of duty is in the way of temptation.
Wesley: had David been at his post at the head of his forces, he had been out of the way of temptation.

debates.question: Was the Ammonite war a single campaign or two?

sides.position: a single spring campaign following the turn of the year
perhaps two, one in autumn and one in spring

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott set the scene — the siesta, the high palace roof overlooking the concealed courts — and read the fall as the fruit of long, intoxicating prosperity.
The bed of sloth proves the bed of lust; Clarke wavers between suspecting design in Bathsheba and clearing her from Eastern custom.
Augustine (via Ellicott): David's fall should put the unfallen on guard and save the fallen from despair.

debates.question: Was Bathsheba's bathing innocent or deliberately immodest?

sides.position: contrived and immodest — could a woman of delicacy so expose herself in view of the king's terrace?
innocent — the court was concealed and no harm suspected; open bathing was common custom

schools.summary: Keil, Ellicott and Clarke fix the identities — Bathsheba/Bath-shua, Eliam/Ammiel — and note Uriah's place among David's thirty heroes, which should have checked him.
Gill: better not to have inquired at all than to feed the desire raised at the sight of her.
Wesley: instead of suppressing the kindled desire, David seeks rather to feed it.

themes: from the eye to the will

schools.summary: Keil, Clarke and Ellicott read the text closely: no force is implied — she came without resistance — yet David is the far greater offender.
Wesley: taken by persuasion, not force; 'all the way to sin is down hill.'
Gill and the company mark the ceremonial 'purified from her uncleanness' as a conscience exact about ritual and dead to the sin.

debates.question: How far is Bathsheba to blame?

sides.position: not free from blame — she yielded without resistance, of little discretion
the far greater offender was David; she was taken by persuasion

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott hear in the two-word message an appeal for concealment, its dark shadow the Law that both adulterers must die (Lev. 20:10).
Gill: the conception is the thing whereby the sin would be discovered, and shame or worse follow.
Henry: the king entrusted to punish adulterers has made himself a pattern where he should have been a terror to evildoers.

themes: the law of death for adultery
the king as judge and offender

schools.summary: Keil (with Ellicott) shows the summons was a pretext — the questions about the war a screen for sending Uriah home to his wife.
Henry: David means to father the child on Uriah, fearing a husband's revenge or rebellion; hope of impunity encourages iniquity.
Gill sets Joab and the army at Rabbah, sixty-four miles off, whence Uriah is recalled.
Keil: the inquiry after Joab, the people and the war is a pretext screening the private design of sending Uriah home.
Gill: Uriah reports to the king before his own house, eager to know the king's special business with him.
Ellicott: the 'mess of meat' is a royal present (as in Gen. 43:34), a choice dish to honour the guest and disarm him.
Clarke and Wesley: the kindness is artful — meant to send Uriah to his wife so the child pass as his; murder is not yet in David's heart.
Gill: 'wash thy feet' is the refreshment David uses to steer him toward his own bed.
Ellicott and Clarke locate Uriah in the palace guard chamber, sleeping with the servants after the manner of Eastern soldiers.
Wesley: Uriah lay at the door by God's secret providence, which would bring David's sin to light; Clarke sees the frank, generous soldier.
Keil: David feigns surprise that Uriah, home from a journey, did not go down; Clarke and Wesley supply the king's show of kindly concern.
Gill: the palace guards report Uriah's abstinence; the king is watching and managing him.
Keil and Ellicott honour Uriah's reasoning — while the ark and army camp in the open field he will not take his ease; the ark went to war as pledge of God's presence.
Clarke calls him brave and disinterested (yet wonders if he suspected); Wesley draws the principle of abstaining from lawful delights while God's people are in danger.
Gill reads the speech as Uriah's apology for a self-denial so unlike the king's indulgence.

debates.question: Did Uriah's refusal spring from suspicion or from principle?

sides.position: from soldierly and godly principle, without suspicion
perhaps from suspicion of what had been done in his absence

schools.summary: Gill: David detains Uriah in his court a second day, having failed to persuade him home.
Henry: the delay marks a sin that will not be abandoned — the beginnings of sin are much to be dreaded, for who knows where they will end.

themes: a sin that will not stop

schools.summary: Ellicott: even drunk, Uriah's resolve governs him — he sleeps again with the servants and will not go down.
Clarke: David made him drunk hoping he would be off his guard and hasten home.
Henry: to make a man drunk on any design is wicked; the prophet's woe (Hab. 2:15) falls on him who gives his neighbour drink.
Gill: David invites Uriah to sup with him as the cover for the attempt.

themes: the woe on him who gives drink

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil: the brave soldier bears his own death-warrant; Keil meditates on how deep a saint may fall when God withdraws his grace.
Henry catalogues the aggravations — deliberate, using Uriah as his own courier, abusing his courage, dragging others into blood-guilt; 'how changed!'
Gill: Satan put the murderous device into David's head and heart.

outliers.note: Adduces the classical tale of Bellerophon carrying letters that sought his own death, yet judges the parallel to Scripture slight and refuses to make the narrative a copy of the fable.

themes: the death-warrant carried by its victim

schools.summary: Keil: the king's wish was fully carried out; Ellicott: yet the 'retire from him' clause was not, so others died and greater blood-guilt fell on David.
Wesley: David seeks to cover one sin with another — how fleshly lusts blind the eyes and destroy all sense of honour and justice.
Gill reads out the murderous orders sent to Joab.

debates.question: Was David's order to 'retire from him' carried out?

sides.position: not carried out — Joab modified it, and more men died as a result
the king's wish was fully carried out by Joab

themes: the innocent do not fall alone
how changed from the man who spared Saul

schools.summary: Ellicott: Joab watched and blockaded the city, arranging his forces to invite a costly sally and posting Uriah within reach of the archers.
Gill: Joab observed where the city's strength and best defences lay, and set Uriah there.

themes: an army bent to hide one sin

schools.summary: Keil: the literal fulfilment does not prove Joab knew the secret — a hard general, he obeyed to serve his own interest another time.
Gill: the city sallied out, as Joab had planned.
Clarke: Uriah, deserted in the assault, cheerfully gave his life for king and country.
Keil: Joab rehearses the courier to report the war 'to the end,' reserving Uriah's death to quell any royal anger at the losses.
Gill: Joab sent word to David as soon as Uriah was killed.
Keil: the courier is coached to withhold Uriah's death until the king's wrath calls for it, though in the event he blurts it all at once.
Gill: Joab directs him what to say at the close, according as he reads the king's countenance.

themes: a message managed to the king's mood
a hero's death as the sweetener

schools.summary: Gill: the king's wrath might show in his countenance or his words, and Joab prepares for it.
Clarke: Joab, perhaps covering a loss of his own, ties the mishap to Uriah's death, which he knew would please the king.

themes: the death that pleases the king

schools.summary: Gill unfolds Jerubbesheth (Gideon) and the death of Abimelech by a woman's millstone at Thebez (Judg. 9:53).
Ellicott: the Abimelech precedent is Joab's cue for the messenger to name Uriah's death without awakening suspicion; it shows how familiar Israel was with its own history.
Keil: the courier condensed Joab's staged report, mentioning Uriah's death at once rather than holding it back.
Gill: the messenger went from Joab and the army before Rabbah to deliver all his charge.

themes: the king not angry at all

schools.summary: Keil reconstructs the tactical report — the sally driven back to the gate, then archers shooting from the wall.
Gill: the particulars of the courier's account, a sally repulsed to the city gate.
Gill: the archers shot from the wall on the servants pressing too near the gate, and among the dead, quietly named last, is Uriah.
Keil hears studied composure in David's reply; Ellicott finds it cunningly worded to feign a special regret for Uriah under a proverb about the impartial sword.
Clarke: abominable hypocrisy — David well knew Uriah's death was no chance of war but his own contrivance.
Gill: David sends the messenger back to Joab with his message of composure.

debates.question: How should David's 'the sword devoureth one as well as another' be read?

sides.position: naked hypocrisy — he knew he had contrived the death
a subtler message, worded to feign special regret for Uriah

themes: the sword devours one as another
the seal of partnership in blood

schools.summary: Ellicott: the usual mourning was seven days (Gen. 50:10; 1 Sam. 31:13), here made as short as decency allowed; Gill notes David hastened the news to hide his sin.
Clarke: she observed the form without the power of sorrow — she lost a captain and got a king.

sides.position: a hollow form — tears forced from a joyful heart
at least the decent observance the Law and custom required

schools.summary: Ellicott: months passed with David's conscience unawakened and Nathan not yet sent — so the sin might be fastened on him beyond denial by the child's birth.
Clarke: the hurried marriage covered the pregnancy, and the clause guards against thinking God indulgent; Wesley: David stayed impenitent for months despite attending God's ordinances.
Gill: David did not wait the customary period; Abarbinel charges the haste as an additional sin.

outliers.note: Closes with an extended peroration defending Scripture's honesty in recording so black a fall, and apportions blame with epigrammatic verdicts on David, Uriah, Joab, and Bathsheba.
